humanities_role_explanation: Includes humanities disciplines like History, Literature, Sociology, and Psychology as part of its multi-discipline coverage, but not designed specifically for humanities analysis
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Multi-modal evaluation combining text questions with images, including a vision-only setting where questions are embedded within screenshots/photos requiring models to process both modalities simultaneously
analytical_tasks_explanation: College-level questions requiring integration of visual and textual information across multiple disciplines, with emphasis on genuine multimodal understanding rather than shortcut exploitation

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates multimodal models across 6 core disciplines and 30 subjects, designed as general evaluation framework

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Tests GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and various open-source models like InternVL2, LLaVA variants, MiniCPM-V2.6

llm_is_primary_system.note: Multimodal LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked for their vision-language understanding capabilities

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes History, Literature, Sociology, Psychology from Humanities and Social Science discipline, plus Art, Art Theory, Design, Music from Art and Design discipline

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses accuracy as primary metric, with performance measured across different settings and compared to human expert performance

published_after_march_2023.note: ArXiv preprint dated May 22, 2025, builds on MMMU which was published in 2024

english_language.note: Paper written in English, though focuses on making benchmark more robust rather than language-specific

summary: MMMU-Pro introduces a more robust version of the MMMU benchmark that filters out text-only solvable questions, augments candidate options from 4 to 10, and introduces a vision-only input setting where questions are embedded in images. The benchmark reveals significant performance drops across all tested models, with decreases ranging from 16.8% to 26.9% compared to original MMMU.
key_findings: All models showed substantial performance drops on MMMU-Pro compared to MMMU, with GPT-4o dropping from 69.1% to 51.9% overall. The vision-only setting proved particularly challenging, with OCR prompts showing minimal improvement. Chain of Thought reasoning helped in technical domains but was less effective in subjective areas like Art and Design. Error analysis showed reasoning errors increased from 26% to 46% of failures.
